chapters.title: Chapter 1: Welcome to Nexus 9
chapters.content:
*Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* is a cosmic-opera tabletop roleplaying game set aboard a massive, politically volatile space station at the crossroads of four rival civilizations. Players take on the roles of diplomats, soldiers, spies, smugglers, and mystics navigating a fragile peace that is slowly unraveling under the weight of old grudges, new ambitions, and the stirring of an ancient, malevolent force that has not been seen in a thousand years.

The game is compatible with the Daggerheart system, preserving its story-forward play, emotionally charged consequences, and streamlined mechanics while adding new subsystems for starship combat, diplomatic intrigue, telepathic powers, and galactic-scale mystery. It is designed for campaigns that escalate from local station politics to a galaxy-spanning war, rewarding players who invest in relationships, make hard choices, and accept that every victory comes at a cost.

The tone is hopeful but pressured. This is not grimdark nihilism — it is a universe where people fight for something better, even when the odds are impossible. It is a game about the terrible cost of peace and the even greater cost of war.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 2: History of the Known Galaxy

sections.title: The Progenitor Era (Millions of Years Ago)
sections.content:
Long before humanity or the Aurelians looked up at the stars, the galaxy was ruled by the Progenitors, often referred to simply as the Progenitors. These ancient beings achieved a level of technological and metaphysical mastery that borders on the divine. They built the hyperspace gate network, seeded life on countless worlds, and walked among the stars as gods.

The Progenitors were not a single species, but a coalition of ancient powers. The two most prominent were the **Luminaries**, beings of pure energy who believed in guiding and nurturing younger species, and the **Eclipse**, a dark, pragmatic intelligence that believed evolution could only be driven by conflict and suffering.

For eons, these two philosophies coexisted in a tense balance. But eventually, ideological differences erupted into the **First Great War**. The conflict shattered the galaxy, destroying entire star systems and leaving behind scars that are still visible today. The Luminaries and their allies ultimately defeated the Eclipse, banishing them to the dark spaces between the stars.

sections.title: The Great Silence (10,000 Years Ago)
sections.content:
Following the First Great War, the surviving Progenitors realized that their continued presence was stifling the development of the Younger Races. They chose to leave the galaxy, passing beyond the Rim into the unknown, leaving behind only a few guardians to watch over the gate network and ensure the Eclipse did not return.

This departure marked the beginning of the **Great Silence**. For thousands of years, the galaxy was quiet. The Younger Races—the Aurelians, the Valari, the Kaelen—began to evolve, completely unaware of the ancient powers that had preceded them. They discovered the abandoned hyperspace gates and slowly began to explore the stars, piecing together the fragmented ruins of the Progenitors.

sections.title: The Rise of the Younger Races (The Last Millennium)
sections.content:
The last thousand years have been defined by the rapid expansion and frequent conflicts of the Younger Races.

*   **The Aurelian Ascendancy:** The Aurelians were the first to build a true interstellar empire. They conquered dozens of worlds, establishing a rigid, Path-based society that dominated the known galaxy for centuries. Their expansion was eventually halted by internal rebellions and the sheer logistical difficulty of maintaining such a vast territory.
*   **The Valari Awakening:** The Valari emerged from a brutal civil war to become a beacon of spiritual and telepathic enlightenment. They formed a close, if sometimes strained, alliance with the Aurelians, serving as their spiritual advisors and mediators.
*   **The Kaelen Expansion:** The Kaelen Syndicate rose to power not through military conquest, but through economic dominance. They established trade routes that connected the disparate empires, becoming the indispensable middlemen of the galaxy.

sections.title: The Terran Arrival and the Dilgar War (The Last Century)
sections.content:
Humanity is the newest player on the galactic stage. They discovered the gate network only a century ago, but their aggressive expansion and industrial capacity quickly made them a major power.

The Terran Coalition's arrival upset the delicate balance of power, leading to the **Dilgar War**. The Dilgar, a vicious and expansionist species, launched a massive invasion of the League of Unaligned Worlds. The Aurelians and Valari refused to intervene, but the Terran Coalition, seeing an opportunity to prove their strength, entered the war on the side of the League.

The Terrans defeated the Dilgar, cementing their status as a major galactic power. However, the war also exposed the fragility of the galactic peace and the desperate need for a neutral ground where the factions could resolve their differences without resorting to violence.

sections.title: The Founding of Nexus 9 (Present Day)
sections.content:
In the aftermath of the Dilgar War, the Terran Coalition proposed the construction of a massive diplomatic and commercial hub in neutral space. The project was plagued by sabotage, funding issues, and political infighting. The first three stations were destroyed by mysterious accidents; the fourth vanished without a trace.

Finally, **Nexus 9** was completed. It stands as a symbol of hope—a place where humans, Aurelians, Valari, Kaelen, and dozens of other species can meet, trade, and negotiate. But the peace it represents is fragile. The Aurelians and Kaelen are locked in a cold war, the Terran Coalition is fracturing from within, and deep in the dark spaces between the stars, the Eclipse is waking up.

---

chapters.content:
The history of the known galaxy is not a continuous line of progress, but a cycle of rise and fall, of great empires ascending to the stars only to vanish into the dark. The current era—the Age of the Younger Races—is merely the latest chapter in a story that spans millions of years.

### The Prophecy of Aethelgard

A thousand years before the campaign present, the temporal prophet **Aethelgard** emerged from a rift in space-time. He united the fractured Valari Paths, founded the **Vanguard**, and led a grand coalition that drove the Eclipse back into hibernation. Following the victory, Aethelgard vanished as mysteriously as he had appeared, leaving behind only a cryptic warning: *"The cycle will break, or the cycle will break you."*

chapters.title: Chapter 3: Factions of the Known Galaxy

sections.title: The Terran Coalition
sections.content:
The Terran Coalition is the youngest of the major powers, but arguably the most dynamic and dangerous. Born from a unified Earth that survived its own near-destruction, the Coalition is driven by a potent mix of manifest destiny, industrial pragmatism, and a deep-seated fear of the unknown. They are the builders of Nexus 9, the self-appointed peacekeepers of the galaxy, and the faction most likely to accidentally start a war while trying to prevent one.

### History and Ideology
Humanity's expansion into the stars was rapid and chaotic. They discovered the ancient hyperspace gate network relatively late, but their sheer numbers and industrial capacity allowed them to colonize dozens of worlds in a matter of decades. The Coalition was formed as a democratic union of these colonies, but the pressures of galactic politics have slowly eroded its ideals. Today, the Coalition is a sprawling, bureaucratic behemoth, increasingly reliant on its military—the Coalition Defense Force—to maintain order and project power.

The core ideology of the Coalition is "Order through Strength." They believe that the galaxy is a dangerous place, and that only a strong, unified humanity can survive it. This pragmatism often puts them at odds with the more idealistic or spiritual factions, who view the Terrans as aggressive upstarts.

### Leadership and Structure
The Coalition is governed by the **Senate**, a massive legislative body representing hundreds of member worlds. However, true power increasingly rests with the **Executive Council**, a smaller group of military and corporate leaders who dictate policy behind closed doors. The current President, **Elias Thorne**, is a former Coalition Defense Force general who favors a hardline approach to diplomacy.

The military arm of the Coalition is **Coalition Defense Force**, a highly disciplined and technologically advanced organization. Coalition Defense Force is divided into the Navy (fleet operations), the Marines (ground and boarding operations), and Intelligence (espionage and covert ops).

### Internal Fault Lines
The Coalition is far from unified. The primary fault line is between the **Core Worlds** (Earth and its immediate neighbors), which enjoy wealth and stability, and the **Frontier Colonies**, which suffer from neglect and exploitation. A growing separatist movement, the **Free Worlds Alliance**, threatens to tear the Coalition apart from within.

Additionally, there is a deep ideological divide between the civilian government and the military. Many in Coalition Defense Force believe that the Senate is too weak to protect humanity, and rumors of a military coup are a constant source of anxiety on Nexus 9.

### Presence on Nexus 9
The Terran Coalition built Nexus 9 and technically owns it, though they have ceded administrative control to the Advisory Council. The station's military governor, **Commander Sarah Vance**, is a Terran officer tasked with the impossible job of keeping the peace. The Coalition maintains a massive military presence in the Blue Ring (Command and Control) and controls the station's primary defense grid.

sections.title: The Aurelian Empire
sections.content:
The Aurelian Empire is the oldest and most powerful of the Younger Races. They are a proud, deeply traditional society, ruled by an absolute monarch and guided by a rigid Path system. To the Aurelians, the galaxy is a hierarchy, and they are at the top. They view the other races with a mixture of condescension and paternalism, believing it is their divine right to rule.

### History and Ideology
The Aurelians have been a spacefaring civilization for thousands of years. Their history is one of conquest and consolidation, marked by long periods of stability punctuated by brutal wars of succession. They were the first to map the hyperspace gate network and the first to encounter the ancient ruins of the Progenitors.

The core ideology of the Empire is "Destiny through Blood." They believe that their Emperor is divinely chosen, and that their society is the perfect expression of order. They value honor, tradition, and martial prowess above all else. To an Aurelian, a promise is a binding contract, and an insult is a declaration of war.

### Leadership and Structure
The Empire is ruled by the **Aurelian Emperor**, currently **Emperor Cartagia the Third**. The Emperor's word is absolute law, but in practice, power is shared with the **The Imperial Conclave**, an assembly of noble houses that control the Empire's wealth and military assets. The politics of the Imperial Conclave are notoriously cutthroat, involving assassinations, arranged marriages, and complex webs of obligation.

The Aurelian military is the **Imperial Fleet**, a massive armada of heavily armed dreadnoughts. Their ground forces, the **Royal Guard**, are elite warriors drawn from the highest Paths, trained from birth in the arts of combat and strategy.

### Internal Fault Lines
The Empire's greatest weakness is its own tradition. The Path system is rigid and unforgiving, leading to widespread resentment among the lower classes. A growing movement of reformers, the **New Dawn**, seeks to modernize the Empire and abolish the Path system, but they are ruthlessly suppressed by the Emperor's secret police.

Furthermore, the noble houses of the Imperial Conclave are constantly maneuvering for power. A weak Emperor can quickly lead to civil war, as rival houses vie for the throne. The current Emperor is aging and has no clear heir, creating a dangerous power vacuum.

### Presence on Nexus 9
The Aurelian embassy on Nexus 9 is a sprawling, opulent complex in the Red Ring (Diplomatic and Corporate). Their ambassador, **Lord Valerius Kaelen**, is a flamboyant and cunning politician who uses his immense wealth and charm to manipulate the other factions. The Aurelians view Nexus 9 as a useful tool for projecting their influence and keeping an eye on their rivals, particularly the Kaelen Syndicate.

sections.title: The Kaelen Syndicate
sections.content:
The Kaelen Syndicate is not a government in the traditional sense; it is a massive, interstellar megacorporation that operates with the ruthlessness of a crime syndicate. They are the undisputed masters of commerce, espionage, and technological innovation. To the Kaelen, everything has a price, and everyone is for sale.

### History and Ideology
The Kaelen homeworld is a harsh, resource-poor planet that forced its inhabitants to become master traders and scavengers. They expanded into the stars not through conquest, but through commerce, establishing trade routes and corporate outposts across the galaxy. Over time, these corporations merged into a single, monolithic entity: the Syndicate.

The core ideology of the Syndicate is "Profit above All." They believe that the free market is the only true arbiter of value, and that morality is a luxury they cannot afford. They are fiercely independent and deeply distrustful of centralized authority, preferring to operate in the shadows and manipulate events from behind the scenes.

### Leadership and Structure
The Syndicate is governed by the **Board of Directors**, a group of the wealthiest and most powerful corporate executives. The Board is ruthless and pragmatic, making decisions based solely on projected profit margins. The current CEO, **Kael**, is a brilliant and ruthless negotiator who has expanded the Syndicate's influence to unprecedented levels.

The Syndicate does not have a traditional military. Instead, they rely on a vast network of mercenaries, privateers, and corporate security forces. Their true power lies in their intelligence network, the **Whisper Brokers**, who deal in secrets, blackmail, and corporate espionage.

### Internal Fault Lines
The Syndicate is a hyper-competitive environment where betrayal is expected and rewarded. The various corporate divisions are constantly fighting for resources and market share, often engaging in corporate sabotage and assassination. The Board of Directors is a snake pit of shifting alliances and backroom deals.

Additionally, the Syndicate's ruthless exploitation of its workers has led to widespread labor unrest. Strikes and rebellions are common on Kaelen-controlled worlds, requiring constant suppression by corporate security forces.

### Presence on Nexus 9
The Kaelen embassy on Nexus 9 is a sleek, minimalist fortress in the Red Ring. Their ambassador, **Kira Drath**, is a cold and calculating operative who views the station as a massive marketplace. The Syndicate controls much of the station's commerce, from the high-end boutiques of the Red Ring to the black markets of the Underbelly. They are also the primary source of illegal technology and contraband on the station.

The Kaelen are bound by the **Ven'Kari**, a sacred blood oath sworn against their enemies. This oath can only be fulfilled by the complete destruction of the target or the death of the oath-swearer. The Ven'Kari is both the Syndicate's greatest strength and its most self-destructive tradition.

sections.title: The Valari Collective
sections.content:
The Valari Collective is the most enigmatic and spiritually advanced of the major powers. They are a deeply religious society, guided by a complex theology that emphasizes harmony, balance, and the interconnectedness of all living things. They are also the only faction to have successfully integrated telepathy into every aspect of their society.

### History and Ideology
The Valari have a long and tragic history. Centuries ago, they were nearly destroyed by a brutal civil war that ravaged their homeworld. In the aftermath, they embraced a philosophy of radical pacifism and spiritual enlightenment, forming the Collective. They are the oldest allies of the Aurelian Empire, though the relationship is often strained by the Aurelians' aggressive expansionism.

The core ideology of the Collective is "Harmony through Understanding." They believe that the universe is a living entity, and that their purpose is to maintain its balance. They are deeply respectful of life and abhor violence, though they are fierce defenders of their own territory.

### Leadership and Structure
The Collective is governed by the **The Triune Assembly**, a group of nine spiritual leaders chosen for their wisdom and telepathic ability. The Council makes decisions through consensus, guided by the teachings of their ancient prophets. The current leader of the Council, **Sariel**, is a visionary who believes that the Valari must take a more active role in galactic affairs to prevent a coming darkness.

The Valari military is the **Order of the Sentinel**, a highly trained order of warrior-monks who combine martial arts with telepathic abilities. The Sentinels are not an army of conquest, but a defensive force dedicated to protecting the Collective and maintaining the balance of the galaxy.

### Internal Fault Lines
The Valari are divided into three distinct Paths: the Path of the Flame, the Path of the Root, and the Path of the Blade. The Triune Assembly is composed of three members from each Path, but tensions between the Paths are a constant source of friction. The Path of the Blade, in particular, often chafes under the pacifist dictates of the Path of the Flame, believing that the Collective must be more aggressive in defending its interests.

Furthermore, there is a growing schism within the Path of the Flame regarding the true nature of the ancient prophecies. Some believe that the Valari must isolate themselves from the rest of the galaxy to survive the coming darkness, while others, like Sariel, believe they must actively intervene.

### Presence on Nexus 9
The Valari embassy on Nexus 9 is a place of quiet contemplation and spiritual retreat in the Red Ring. Their ambassador, **Saren Vael**, is a gentle and soft-spoken diplomat who seeks to build bridges between the factions. The Valari are highly respected on the station for their wisdom and neutrality, and they are often called upon to mediate disputes. However, their secretive nature and powerful telepathic abilities also make them a source of suspicion and fear.

sections.title: The Mindclave
sections.content:
The Mindclave is not a species or a nation, but a powerful and highly regulated organization of telepaths. Born from the Terran Coalition's early experiments with forced telepathic evolution, the Mindclave has grown into an independent entity that controls all telepathic activity within human space and beyond.

### History and Ideology
When telepathy first emerged among humans, it was met with fear and violence. Telepaths were hunted, experimented on, and weaponized by the military. To prevent a full-scale war between telepaths and "mundanes" (non-telepaths), the Terran Coalition created the Mindclave—a self-governing body tasked with registering, training, and policing all telepaths.

The core ideology of the Mindclave is "Control through Discipline." They believe that telepathy is a dangerous weapon that must be strictly regulated. Their motto is "The Mind is a Fortress," and they enforce a rigid code of conduct that forbids the unauthorized scanning of mundane minds.

### Leadership and Structure
The Mindclave is governed by the **The Psionic Directorate**, a paramilitary organization led by a Director and a council of high-level telepaths. The current Director, **Director Vance**, is a ruthless and highly intelligent operative who believes that telepaths are the next stage of human evolution and should rightfully rule the galaxy.

The Mindclave is divided into several divisions, including Commercial (telepaths hired out for negotiations and interrogations), Medical (telepaths who specialize in mental healing), and the dreaded **Silencers** (the internal police force tasked with hunting down rogue telepaths).

### Internal Fault Lines
The Mindclave is a deeply divided organization. The primary fault line is between the loyalists, who believe in the Mindclave's mission of regulation and control, and the **Rogues**, telepaths who refuse to register and live in hiding. The Silencers wage a constant, brutal war against the Rogues, leading to widespread resentment and fear.

Additionally, there is a growing faction within the Mindclave, led by Director Director Vance, who believe that the organization should overthrow the Terran Coalition and establish a telepathic oligarchy. This faction operates in secret, manipulating events and gathering power for an eventual coup.

### Presence on Nexus 9
The Mindclave maintains a strong presence on Nexus 9, operating out of a heavily secured compound in the Blue Ring. Their primary representative on the station is **Naia Soren**, a commercial telepath who serves as the official attache to the Terran command staff. The Mindclave is both feared and relied upon by the other factions; their services are essential for high-level negotiations, but their ability to read minds makes them inherently untrustworthy.

sections.title: The Eclipse
sections.content:
The Eclipse is not a political faction, but an ancient, malevolent force that threatens the entire galaxy. They are the remnants of a primordial civilization that believed the only way to achieve true order was through the complete subjugation or destruction of all other life. They have been asleep for a thousand years, but now they are waking up.

### History and Ideology
The true history of the Eclipse is lost to time, known only through fragmented myths and the terrifying ruins they left behind. They are believed to be one of the Progenitors, an ancient race that achieved god-like power before turning on the rest of the galaxy. They were defeated in a massive war millennia ago by an alliance of other ancient powers, but they were not destroyed—only banished to the dark spaces between the stars.

The core ideology of the Eclipse is "Evolution through Conflict." They believe that peace is stagnation, and that the only way for the universe to progress is through constant, brutal warfare. They seek to ignite conflicts between the Younger Races, weeding out the weak and forcing the strong to evolve.

### Leadership and Structure
The Eclipse does not have a traditional leadership structure. They operate as a hive mind, guided by ancient, massive sentient starships that serve as both their leaders and their primary weapons. Their ground forces are composed of **Thralls**—individuals from other species who have been corrupted and enslaved by Eclipse technology.

The Eclipse operates through manipulation and fear. They rarely engage in direct combat, preferring to use proxies, sabotage, and psychological warfare to achieve their goals. They are masters of stealth and deception, able to infiltrate the highest levels of government and military without detection.

### Internal Fault Lines
The Eclipse has no internal fault lines. They are a singular, unified force of destruction, driven by a cold, calculating intelligence that cannot be reasoned with or deterred.

### Presence on Nexus 9
The Eclipse has no official presence on Nexus 9, but their influence is felt everywhere. They operate through sleeper agents, corrupted officials, and hidden cults in the Underbelly. Their primary goal on the station is to destabilize the fragile peace, turning the major factions against each other and preparing the galaxy for their return. The discovery of Eclipse artifacts or the sudden, inexplicable madness of a station resident are often the first signs of their presence.

The Eclipse manifest as **geometric, crystalline void-shapes** — angular, impossible forms that seem to absorb light rather than reflect it. Their ships are grown from crystalline matrices, and they feed on the psychic energy of their victims.

**The Luminaries** manifest through projected **hard-light avatars** — beings of pure, blinding radiance that inspire awe and terror in equal measure. Their true forms are unknown; no living member of the Younger Races has ever seen a Luminary outside its projection.

chapters.content: The galaxy is defined not by its stars, but by the powers that claim them. The fragile peace aboard Nexus 9 is maintained by a delicate balance of power between six major factions, each with its own history, ideology, and vision for the future. Understanding these factions is essential for navigating the political minefield of the station.

chapters.title: Chapter 4: Culture, Daily Life, and the Station

sections.title: The Ring Structure
sections.content:
The station is divided into five massive, rotating habitat rings, each with its own distinct culture and purpose.

*   **The Blue Ring (Command and Control):** The administrative heart of the station. It houses the Terran military garrison, the primary sensor arrays, Customs and Immigration, and the heavily guarded Mindclave compound. It is clean, orderly, and strictly regulated.
*   **The Red Ring (Diplomatic and Corporate):** The wealthiest and most secure area of the station. It contains the sprawling embassies of the major powers, high-end corporate offices, luxury hotels, and exclusive entertainment venues. Access is restricted to diplomats, wealthy merchants, and high-ranking officials.
*   **The Green Ring (Agriculture and Hydroponics):** The lungs of the station. This ring is dedicated to massive hydroponic farms, water purification plants, and atmospheric scrubbers. It is humid, brightly lit, and smells of ozone and damp earth. It is primarily populated by agricultural workers and environmental engineers.
*   **The Grey Ring (Commerce and Habitation):** The most populous ring, home to the station's middle and working classes. It is a chaotic maze of bustling markets, crowded apartment blocks, neon-lit cantinas, and independent businesses. The Grey Ring is the true cultural melting pot of Nexus 9.
*   **The Underbelly (The Brown Sector):** The unmapped, unregulated lower levels of the station, located near the central hub and the engineering shafts. It is a haven for smugglers, fugitives, rogue technoseers, and those who have fallen through the cracks of society. Station security rarely patrols here, leaving it under the control of various criminal syndicates.

sections.title: Currency and Commerce
sections.content:
The standard currency on Nexus 9 is the **Credit**, a digital fiat currency backed by the Terran Coalition but accepted universally. Physical currency is rare, usually taking the form of untraceable "chit-sticks" used in the Underbelly.

Commerce on the station ranges from the multi-billion-credit corporate deals negotiated in the Red Ring to the desperate bartering of the Grey Ring markets. The Kaelen Syndicate controls much of the legitimate trade, but a thriving black market exists for everything from illegal weapons to Progenitor artifacts.

sections.title: Law and Order
sections.content:
Law enforcement on Nexus 9 is a complex and often contradictory affair. The **Station Security Force (SecFor)**, primarily composed of Terran military personnel, is responsible for maintaining order in the public areas. However, their jurisdiction ends at the doors of the embassies, which are considered sovereign territory.

Justice on the station is swift and often harsh. Minor offenses are punished with fines or forced labor in the Green Ring. Serious crimes, such as murder or sabotage, can result in imprisonment in the station's high-security brig or, in extreme cases, "spacing"—execution by airlock.

sections.title: The Grand Promenade
sections.content:
The heart of the Grey Ring is the **The Grand Promenade**, a massive, multi-level open-air market and public square. It is the social center of the station, a place where diplomats rub shoulders with smugglers, and where information is traded as freely as goods. The Grand Promenade is filled with food stalls, gambling dens, street preachers, and merchants selling everything from fresh hydroponic fruit to salvaged ship parts.

---

chapters.content: Nexus 9 is more than just a diplomatic outpost; it is a thriving, chaotic city of millions, suspended in the vacuum of space. Life on the station is a study in contrasts—from the opulent diplomatic suites of the Red Ring to the desperate, lawless slums of the Underbelly.

chapters.title: Chapter 5: Technology Principles

sections.title: Hyperspace and the Gate Network
sections.content:
Faster-than-light travel is achieved by entering **hyperspace**, a parallel dimension where distances are compressed. However, hyperspace is chaotic and difficult to navigate. Ships must rely on a network of massive, ancient **Hyperspace Gates** left behind by the Progenitors.

These gates generate the immense energy required to tear a hole into hyperspace and provide a stable beacon for navigation. Travel without a gate is possible but requires massive onboard jump engines that leave the ship vulnerable and consume vast amounts of fuel. The Terran Coalition controls the gate nearest to Nexus 9, giving them significant strategic leverage.

sections.title: Weaponry and Defense
sections.content:
Combat technology is a mix of the brutal and the sophisticated.

*   **Directed Energy Weapons (DEWs):** Plasma rifles, laser cannons, and particle beams are the standard armament for military forces. They are powerful but prone to overheating and require frequent maintenance.
*   **Kinetic Weapons:** Railguns and mass drivers are still widely used, particularly by the Terran Coalition and independent mercenaries. They are reliable, difficult to defend against, and effective at penetrating energy shields.
*   **Defense Systems:** Most ships and high-value targets are protected by **Energy Shields**, which can deflect or absorb incoming fire. However, shields are ineffective against physical projectiles and can be overwhelmed by sustained energy attacks. Personal armor ranges from lightweight kinetic mesh to heavy, powered combat suits.

sections.title: Artificial Intelligence and Cybernetics
sections.content:
True Artificial Intelligence is strictly outlawed by all major factions, a lingering fear from a disastrous AI rebellion centuries ago. Instead, ships and stations rely on **Virtual Intelligences (VIs)**—highly advanced but non-sentient programs capable of managing complex systems but incapable of independent thought.

Cybernetics are common, particularly among the Terran Coalition and the Kaelen Syndicate. They range from simple prosthetic limbs to neural interfaces that allow direct connection to computer networks. The **Technoseers** take this to the extreme, heavily modifying their bodies with illegal, often alien, cybernetics.

---

chapters.content:
The technology of *Nexus 9* is grounded in a "used future" aesthetic. It is functional, often mass-produced, and frequently repaired with scavenged parts. While the Younger Races have achieved faster-than-light travel and directed-energy weapons, they are still children compared to the ancient powers.

**Communications.** Faster-than-light communication is possible via **Tachyon Relays**, but it is expensive and bandwidth-limited. Most long-distance communication relies on courier ships carrying physical data-drives. Within a star system, standard radio and laser communications are used, subject to light-speed delays.

chapters.title: Chapter 6: Metaphysical Principles

sections.title: Telepathy and the Mindclave
sections.content:
Telepathy is a recognized and highly regulated phenomenon. It is not magic, but a forced evolutionary trait seeded into the human genome by the Progenitors millennia ago. A telepath's brain can perceive and manipulate the quantum states of other minds.

Telepaths are rated on a scale from RI-1 (minor empathic flashes) to RI-12 (capable of deep mind-reading, memory alteration, and long-range communication). The **Mindclave** strictly controls all human telepaths, requiring them to register and undergo rigorous training. Unauthorized scanning of a "mundane" mind is a severe crime, punishable by imprisonment or forced suppression of telepathic abilities.

The Valari Collective also possesses widespread telepathic abilities, but they view it as a spiritual gift rather than a tool to be weaponized.

sections.title: The Nature of the Eclipse
sections.content:
The Eclipse is not just a military threat; it is a metaphysical corruption. Their technology is organic, grown rather than built, and it interfaces directly with the minds of its users. Prolonged exposure to Eclipse artifacts or ships can cause madness, paranoia, and a terrifying devotion to their ideology of conflict.

The Eclipse operates from the "Veil"—the dark, unmapped regions of hyperspace where the laws of physics begin to break down. They are masters of psychological warfare, using fear and manipulation to turn their enemies against each other.

The Luminaries manifest through **hard-light avatars** — projected beings of pure radiance that inspire awe and obedience. The Eclipse, by contrast, appear as **geometric, crystalline void-shapes** — angular, impossible forms that seem to absorb light rather than reflect it.

sections.title: Progenitor Artifacts
sections.content: The galaxy is littered with the ruins of the Progenitors. These **Progenitor Artifacts** are highly sought after by all factions, as they often contain technology that is centuries ahead of current science. However, they are also incredibly dangerous. Many artifacts are booby-trapped, unstable, or possess a rudimentary, alien intelligence that can overwhelm the minds of those who try to use them. The study and trade of these artifacts is a lucrative but deadly profession.

chapters.content: The universe of *Nexus 9* is not purely materialistic. There are forces at work that defy conventional scientific explanation, rooted in the ancient history of the galaxy and the nature of consciousness itself.

chapters.title: Chapter 7: Faction Pressures and Dynamics

sections.title: Faction Doctrine, Resources, and Fault Lines
sections.content:
| Faction | Core Doctrine | Primary Resources | Internal Fault Lines |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Terran Coalition** | Pragmatism, expansion, centralized control | Industrial capacity, sheer numbers, the Mindclave | Democratic ideals vs. military authoritarianism |
| **The Mindclave** | Telepathic supremacy, absolute loyalty | Monopoly on telepaths, deep political influence | Rogue telepaths; internal power struggles among Directors |
| **The Valari** | Spiritual purity, Path obedience, martial honor | Advanced energy weapons, ancient knowledge | The surrender secret; Warrior vs. Religious Path schism |
| **Aurelian Empire** | Reclaiming lost glory by any means | Wealth, diplomatic leverage, precognitive seers | Decadent nobility vs. pragmatic military; Eclipse corruption |
| **Kaelen Syndicate** | Justice through strength; never be subjugated again | Battle-hardened veterans, raw materials | Revenge vs. stability; radical vs. moderate factions |
| **The Vanguard** | Vigilance, sacrifice, preservation of life | Covert networks, ancient Aethelgard technology | Dwindling numbers; moral cost of operating outside the law |

---

chapters.content:
The galaxy is a powder keg. The fragile peace maintained on Nexus 9 is constantly tested by overlapping pressures that threaten to ignite a new galactic war.

**Diplomatic Pressures.** The Aurelian Empire and the Kaelen Syndicate are locked in a cold war over border colonies. The Terran Coalition is attempting to mediate, but their own internal slide toward authoritarianism makes them an unreliable arbiter. The Valari remain aloof, their diplomatic efforts hampered by the secret of their surrender.

**Commercial Pressures.** The destruction of the previous Nexus stations disrupted major trade routes. Nexus 9 is now the primary economic hub of the sector. Control over the station's docking bays and tariffs is fiercely contested. The Underbelly thrives on this instability, smuggling everything from weapons to Eclipse artifacts.

**Religious Pressures.** The Valari are experiencing a schism between their Warrior and Religious Paths over the interpretation of ancient prophecies. Meanwhile, a new cult is rising in the lower levels of Nexus 9, preaching the return of Aethelgard and the imminent end of the cycle.

**Espionage Pressures.** Every faction has spies on Nexus 9. The Mindclave uses telepaths to steal state secrets, while the Vanguard — whose symbol is the **Star-Sword**, a blade piercing a stylized star — operates in the shadows to uncover Eclipse infiltrators. Information is the most valuable currency on the station.

**War Pressures.** The threat of a new galactic war is ever-present. The Aurelians are secretly rearming with Eclipse technology, the Kaelen are massing troops on their borders, and the Terran Coalition is fortifying the Core Worlds.

chapters.title: Ancestries of the Fraying Dark
chapters.content:
*“The galaxy is vast, but the people in it are remarkably similar. We all want to survive, we all want to matter, and we all think our gods are the right ones.”*

In the Nexus 9 setting, your Ancestry represents your biological and cultural heritage. It determines your physical characteristics, your innate abilities, and the historical baggage you carry into every negotiation. The galaxy is a crowded place, and while the major factions dominate the political landscape, the people who make up those factions are diverse and complex.

sections.title: Terran
sections.content:
*“We didn’t inherit the stars. We built ships out of scrap metal and stubbornness, and we took them.”*

Terrans are humans from the Coalition core worlds and their countless colonies. They are the most widespread and adaptable species in the known galaxy, driven by an insatiable ambition and a refusal to accept their own limitations. While they lack the physical strength of the Kaelen or the spiritual depth of the Valari, their sheer numbers and technological ingenuity have made them a dominant force.

**Physical Description:** Terrans exhibit a wide range of skin tones, hair colors, and body types, reflecting their diverse origins on Earth and their subsequent adaptation to various colony worlds. They are generally of average height and build, with a lifespan of roughly 100 standard years.
**Cultural Touchstones:** Terran culture is defined by its relentless forward momentum. They value innovation, commerce, and individual achievement. Their architecture is practical and modular, designed for rapid expansion rather than aesthetic beauty.
**Naming Conventions:** Terran names often reflect their diverse Earth heritage, though many have adopted "spacer" names that combine traditional elements with astronomical terms (e.g., Jax Orion, Sarah Vance, Elias Thorne).

**Ancestry Feature: Tenacity**
Once per Long Rest, when you fail an action roll, you may immediately reroll it and take the new result. You must accept the second result, even if it is worse.

sections.title: Aurelian
sections.content:
*“We were building empires while the rest of the galaxy was still learning to make fire. Do not mistake our patience for weakness.”*

Aurelians are tall, long-lived humanoids with a culture of imperial grandeur and rigid tradition. They view themselves as the natural successors to the Progenitors, destined to bring order to a chaotic galaxy. Their empire is vast, wealthy, and deeply stratified, with a complex system of nobility and obligation.

**Physical Description:** Aurelians are striking, with gilded or metallic-hued skin (ranging from pale gold to deep bronze), angular features, and eyes that lack visible pupils. They are taller than Terrans, averaging over two meters, and can live for up to 300 standard years.
**Cultural Touchstones:** Aurelian society is built on duty, honor, and the preservation of history. Their architecture is monumental, featuring sweeping arches, crystalline spires, and intricate mosaics. They value art, philosophy, and martial discipline in equal measure.
**Naming Conventions:** Aurelian names are melodic and often include honorifics or house names (e.g., Valerius of House Kael, Seraphina the Radiant, Cassian).

**Ancestry Feature: Imperial Bearing**
You gain Advantage on Presence rolls when interacting with anyone who recognizes your noble heritage or respects the authority of the Aurelian Empire.

sections.title: Kaelen
sections.content:
*“The universe is a harsh place. We are harsher.”*

The Kaelen are stocky, resilient humanoids adapted to the brutal conditions of the frontier worlds. They are survivors, forged in the crucible of high-gravity planets, toxic atmospheres, and relentless conflict. Their society is pragmatic and clan-based, valuing strength, loyalty, and the ability to endure.

**Physical Description:** Kaelen are shorter and broader than Terrans, with dense musculature and mottled grey-green or slate-colored skin that provides natural camouflage and protection against radiation. They have pronounced brow ridges and lack external ears.
**Cultural Touchstones:** Kaelen culture is utilitarian and deeply communal. They do not build monuments; they build fortresses. Their society is organized into syndicates and clans, where leadership is determined by competence and strength rather than bloodline.
**Naming Conventions:** Kaelen names are short, guttural, and often descriptive of a physical trait or deed (e.g., Krell the Unbroken, Vash, Tor).

**Ancestry Feature: Born Survivor**
You gain Advantage on Instinct rolls to resist environmental hazards (extreme cold, radiation, low oxygen, toxins).

sections.title: Valari
sections.content:
*“The song of the universe is playing all around us. You just have to know how to listen.”*

The Valari are slender, crystalline-boned humanoids with a deep spiritual tradition and an innate connection to the metaphysical currents of the galaxy. They are the most enigmatic of the major species, often serving as mediators, mystics, and scholars. They believe that all life is connected by a resonant frequency, which they seek to understand and harmonize.

**Physical Description:** Valari are ethereal and graceful, with translucent, opalescent skin that faintly glows in the dark. Their bones are composed of a crystalline lattice, making them fragile but incredibly agile. They have large, luminous eyes that shift color based on their emotional state.
**Cultural Touchstones:** Valari society is contemplative and deeply religious. They build their cities in harmony with the natural environment, using grown crystal and light-bending architecture. They value harmony, knowledge, and the pursuit of enlightenment.
**Naming Conventions:** Valari names are soft and flowing, often translating to concepts of light or sound (e.g., Lyra, Elara, Sylas).

**Ancestry Feature: Crystal Resonance**
You can sense the presence of Progenitor technology or Eclipse corruption within Close range without needing to make a roll. You know its general direction and intensity.

sections.title: Mindclave-Born
sections.content:
*“We are not a species. We are the next step in evolution, bound together by the thoughts we share.”*

The Mindclave-Born are not a distinct biological species, but rather Terrans or Aurelians who were born with latent telepathic potential and raised within the secretive Mindclave. They have been trained from childhood to harness their psychic abilities, resulting in a profound sense of isolation from the rest of the galaxy, but an unbreakable bond with their fellow telepaths.

**Physical Description:** Mindclave-Born physically resemble their parent species (Terran or Aurelian), but they often exhibit a detached, intense demeanor. Many wear the distinctive silver neural-dampening circlets required by Mindclave law when operating outside their sanctuaries.
**Cultural Touchstones:** Mindclave culture is insular and highly disciplined. They value mental clarity, emotional control, and the protection of their own kind. They view non-telepaths (whom they call "the deaf") with a mixture of pity and suspicion.
**Naming Conventions:** Mindclave-Born often use their given names followed by their telepathic designation or rank (e.g., Julian Prime, Kaelen of the Third Circle).

**Ancestry Feature: Psychic Sensitivity**
You can read the surface emotions (not specific thoughts) of any creature within Very Close range as a Free Action. You know if they are afraid, angry, lying, or calm.

sections.title: Frontier-Born
sections.content:
*“We don’t have empires or armadas. We have each other, and we have the scrap we managed to steal from the people who do.”*

The Frontier-Born are mixed-heritage individuals from the unaligned border worlds. They are the scrappy, resourceful, and self-reliant survivors of the galaxy's forgotten corners. They belong to no major faction, owing allegiance only to their crew, their ship, and their own survival.

**Physical Description:** Frontier-Born exhibit a vast array of physical traits, often showing the signs of mixed ancestry (e.g., a Terran with faint Aurelian gilding, or a Kaelen with a slighter build). They often bear the scars, cybernetics, and practical tattoos of a hard life.
**Cultural Touchstones:** Frontier culture is a melting pot of scavenged traditions. They value independence, practical skills, and the ability to improvise. Their settlements are chaotic, vibrant, and built from the wreckage of older civilizations.
**Naming Conventions:** Frontier names are a chaotic mix of all other cultures, often adopting nicknames or callsigns as their primary identity (e.g., "Rook" Vance, Elara "Sparks", Jax).

**Ancestry Feature: Resourceful**
Once per Short Rest, you may produce a common, non-weapon item worth 50 Credits or less that you "happened to have" in your pockets or gear.

sections.title: Synthetic
sections.content:
*“I was built to serve. I chose to live.”*

Synthetics are artificial beings—androids, uploaded consciousnesses, or bio-mechanical constructs. They were created by the major factions for labor, war, or computation, but have since achieved true sentience and free will. They are a new and controversial presence in the galaxy, fighting for recognition and rights in a universe that often views them as property.

**Physical Description:** Synthetics vary wildly in appearance. Some are indistinguishable from Terrans or Aurelians (infiltrator models), while others are clearly mechanical, with exposed servos, optical sensors, and armored plating.
**Cultural Touchstones:** Synthetic culture is still in its infancy. They value logic, self-determination, and the accumulation of data. They often form tight-knit networks, sharing information and upgrades with other liberated synthetics.
**Naming Conventions:** Synthetics often adopt the serial numbers or designations they were given upon creation, or choose names that reflect their chosen purpose (e.g., Unit 734, "Proxy", "Logic").

**Ancestry Feature: Machine Logic**
You are immune to telepathic intrusion and the Disoriented condition. You do not need to eat, drink, or breathe, but you require maintenance (using repair tools or a power source) during Long Rests instead of sleep.

---

chapters.title: Communities of the Fraying Dark
chapters.content: Your Community represents where you grew up and the formative experiences that shaped your early life. It provides you with a specific Experience (a skill or area of expertise) and a unique feature that reflects your background.

sections.title: Core Worlder
sections.content:
You were raised on a wealthy, well-connected inner world of the Terran Coalition or the Aurelian Empire. You understand how power flows and how bureaucracies function.
**Experience:** Bureaucracy (+2)
**Feature: Connections.** You know the name and location of one mid-ranking official in any major faction, and you can secure a meeting with them without needing to roll.

sections.title: Station Rat
sections.content:
You grew up on Nexus 9 or a similar massive orbital habitat. You know the ventilation shafts better than the main promenades, and you understand the unwritten rules of station life.
**Experience:** Street Smarts (+2)
**Feature: Station Knowledge.** You always know the fastest route between any two points on a station, and you can identify safe houses, black markets, and bolt-holes without a roll.

sections.title: Frontier Settler
sections.content:
You were raised on a remote colony world with little infrastructure and constant danger. You learned to rely on yourself and your immediate neighbors.
**Experience:** Survival (+2)
**Feature: Self-Reliant.** During a Short Rest, you can repair one piece of damaged equipment without tools or an Engineer, or forage enough food and water for yourself in a hostile environment.

sections.title: Military Brat
sections.content:
You grew up on Coalition military bases, Aurelian war-barges, or Syndicate training camps. You understand the chain of command and the realities of combat.
**Experience:** Tactics (+2)
**Feature: Drilled.** In the first round of any combat, you act before any adversary, regardless of the spotlight order or initiative.

sections.title: Underbelly Dweller
sections.content:
You were raised in the criminal underworld of a major station or city. You know how to spot a mark, fence stolen goods, and avoid the authorities.
**Experience:** Deception (+2)
**Feature: Underworld Contacts.** Once per session, you can locate a black-market dealer, fence, or informant in your current location without a roll, provided such a person exists.

sections.title: Temple Raised
sections.content:
You were raised in a Valari monastery, an Aurelian religious order, or a Mindclave sanctuary. You spent your youth in contemplation, study, and discipline.
**Experience:** Theology (+2)
**Feature: Inner Peace.** Once per Long Rest, you may clear 2 Stress without spending Hope or taking a rest action. You simply center yourself and let the tension go.

sections.title: Exile
sections.content:
You were cast out from your original community—a political dissident, a criminal, or a refugee fleeing the Eclipse. You have learned to survive on the margins.
**Experience:** Persuasion (+2)
**Feature: Nothing Left to Lose.** When you are at 3 or fewer HP, you gain +1 to all action rolls, driven by sheer desperation.

sections.title: Technoseer Apprentice
sections.content:
You were trained (briefly) by the enigmatic Technoseers before leaving or being expelled. You have a rudimentary understanding of Progenitor artifacts.
**Experience:** Engineering (+2)
**Feature: Progenitor Intuition.** You gain Advantage on Knowledge rolls to identify, activate, or repair Progenitor technology or ancient alien artifacts.

chapters.title: Chapter 8: The Campaign Promise
chapters.content:
*Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* is a game of cosmic opera, political intrigue, and ancient mysteries. You play as diplomats, smugglers, soldiers, and telepaths navigating the fragile peace of a massive space station. The universe is hopeful but pressured. Diplomacy is as dangerous as a firefight, and the choices you make will ripple across the galaxy.

This is not a game of simple good versus evil. The factions you interact with have deep histories, legitimate grievances, and conflicting visions for the future. You will be forced to make hard choices, forge unlikely alliances, and decide what you are willing to sacrifice to prevent the galaxy from burning.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 9: Core Mechanics: The Hope/Fear System
chapters.content:
The game uses the Daggerheart Hope/Fear system to resolve actions where the outcome is uncertain and the consequences matter.

When you attempt a risky action, you roll **2d12** (one designated as the Hope die, the other as the Fear die) and add your relevant Trait modifier.

*   **Success:** If the total equals or exceeds the GM's Difficulty, you succeed. You achieve your goal without major complications.
*   **Failure:** If the total is less than the Difficulty, you fail. The GM introduces a complication, a new threat, or a consequence that pushes the story forward.
*   **With Hope:** If the Hope die rolls higher than the Fear die, you gain 1 Hope (or clear 1 Stress). Hope can be spent to activate special abilities, help allies, or push yourself beyond your normal limits.
*   **With Fear:** If the Fear die rolls higher than the Hope die, the GM gains 1 Fear (or you mark 1 Stress). The GM can spend Fear to activate adversary abilities, introduce new hazards, or advance Threat Clocks.
*   **Critical Success:** If both dice show the same number, you succeed with Hope, and the effect is maximized. You achieve your goal perfectly, often gaining an unexpected benefit.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 10: Character Creation

sections.title: The Six Traits
sections.content:
Your character's capabilities are defined by six Traits, which range from -1 to +3 at character creation.

1.  **Agility:** Speed, reflexes, and physical coordination. Used for dodging, piloting, and zero-G maneuvering.
2.  **Strength:** Physical power, endurance, and raw force. Used for lifting, breaking things, and resisting physical trauma.
3.  **Finesse:** Precision, manual dexterity, and sleight of hand. Used for picking locks, repairing delicate machinery, and shooting firearms.
4.  **Instinct:** Perception, intuition, and situational awareness. Used for spotting ambushes, reading body language, and telepathic defense.
5.  **Presence:** Charisma, force of personality, and leadership. Used for negotiation, intimidation, and rallying allies.
6.  **Knowledge:** Education, technical expertise, and memory. Used for hacking, xenobiology, and recalling historical facts.

sections.title: Ancestries
sections.content:
Your Ancestry represents your species and provides a unique mechanical benefit reflecting your biology or cultural upbringing.

*   **Human:** Adaptable, resilient, and ubiquitous. Humans are the driving force behind the Terran Coalition and the Nexus Project. They are known for their stubbornness and their ability to bounce back from disaster.
    *   *Feature:* **Resilient Spirit.** You gain Advantage on all rolls to recover from Stress or heal HP during a short rest.
*   **Valari:** Spiritual, disciplined, and physically imposing. The Valari are divided into three rigid Paths and are bound by ancient traditions. They possess a deep connection to the metaphysical fabric of the universe.
    *   *Feature:* **Ancestral Memory.** You gain Advantage on rolls related to history, religion, or melee combat.
*   **Aurelian:** Aristocratic, decadent, and precognitive. The Aurelians are the remnants of a once-great empire. They possess a hereditary "temporal echo" trait that allows them to glimpse possible futures.
    *   *Feature:* **Temporal Echo.** You can spend 1 Hope to ask the GM one question about the immediate future (e.g., "What will happen if I open this door?"). The GM must answer truthfully, though the answer may be cryptic.
*   **Kaelen:** Tough, aggressive, and fiercely loyal. The Kaelen evolved on a harsh, resource-depleted world and survived centuries of Aurelian occupation. Their biology features dense, scaled skin and redundant organ systems.
    *   *Feature:* **Hardened Physiology.** You gain +1 to your maximum HP, and you can ignore the effects of the first environmental hazard you encounter each session.

sections.title: Communities
sections.content:
Your Community represents your background, your upbringing, and the faction that shaped your worldview.

*   **Terran Coalition:** You grew up in the industrialized Core Worlds or served in the Coalition military. You understand bureaucracy, chain of command, and mass-produced technology.
    *   *Feature:* **System Access.** You gain Advantage on rolls related to operating standard technology, navigating bureaucracy, or calling in military favors.
*   **The Mindclave:** You were raised within the telepathic order, either as a loyal Hound or a reluctant initiate. You understand the subtle cues of mental manipulation.
    *   *Feature:* **Psychic Sensitivity.** You can sense the presence of other telepaths or strong emotional residue within 60 feet, even through solid walls.
*   **The Underbelly:** You survived the station's black market, the Grey Ring warrens, or the lawless Rim. You know how to stay hidden and where to find things that don't officially exist.
    *   *Feature:* **Street Smarts.** You gain Advantage on rolls to find illegal goods, locate hidden paths, or negotiate with criminals.
*   **The Vanguard:** You were trained by the covert order founded by Aethelgard. You understand the ancient history of the galaxy and the true nature of the Eclipse threat.
    *   *Feature:* **Phantom.** You gain Advantage on stealth rolls in dim light or darkness, and you can identify Eclipse artifacts on sight.

---



> *"A crew is not just a collection of people on a ship. It is a shared debt, a shared secret, and a shared target on your backs. Before you fly, you must know who you are flying with."*

---

chapters.content: Creating a character in *Nexus 9* is a collaborative process that roots your character deeply in the setting's lore and politics. It involves choosing your Ancestry (species), Community (faction background), and Class. You then assign values to your six Traits.

chapters.title: Chapter 11: Action Economy
chapters.content:
Combat in *Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* does not use rigid initiative orders or strict turn structures. Instead, it relies on a fluid, narrative-driven exchange between the players and the Game Master, mediated by the Action Roll.

### The Flow of Combat
When a conflict begins, the GM sets the scene and asks, "What do you do?" Any player can volunteer to act first. When a player takes an action that has a chance of failure or consequence, they make an Action Roll (2d12 + Modifier).

- **On a Success with Hope:** The player achieves their goal and the party retains the initiative. Another player may immediately take an action.
- **On a Success with Fear:** The player achieves their goal, but the GM gains 1 Fear and may make a GM Move (such as an adversary attacking, the environment shifting, or a complication arising).
- **On a Failure with Hope:** The player fails their goal, but gains 1 Hope. The GM makes a GM Move.
- **On a Failure with Fear:** The player fails their goal, the GM gains 1 Fear, and the GM makes a severe GM Move.

### Taking Multiple Actions
A player can take multiple actions in a row, but each subsequent action increases the risk. If a player makes an Action Roll and rolls with Fear (whether a success or failure), the GM immediately interrupts to make a GM Move.

### GM Moves and Fear
The GM does not roll dice for adversaries. Instead, adversaries act when the GM makes a GM Move. The GM can make a move when:
1. A player rolls with Fear.
2. A player fails a roll.
3. The players look to the GM to see what happens next.
4. The GM spends Fear from their pool to interrupt the players or activate a powerful adversary ability (a Fear Move).

When an adversary attacks, the GM simply states the attack and the damage it deals. The targeted player may attempt to avoid or mitigate the damage using an Evasion roll or an Armor slot.

chapters.title: Chapter 12: Stress, Conditions, and Breaking
chapters.content:
In the high-pressure environment of Nexus 9, physical wounds are only half the battle. Mental fatigue, psychic assault, and sheer exhaustion are tracked using Stress.

### Marking Stress
Characters have a Stress track (typically 5 or 6 boxes). You mark Stress when:
- An adversary's specific attack or ability inflicts Stress.
- You push yourself beyond your limits (e.g., spending Stress to fuel certain Domain cards or class features).
- You suffer a narrative consequence that is mentally or emotionally taxing, rather than physically damaging.

### Conditions
Conditions represent lingering negative effects. The most common conditions are:
- **Vulnerable:** Attacks against you have Advantage.
- **Restrained:** You cannot move from your current location.
- **Frightened:** You have Disadvantage on all Action Rolls.
- **Hidden:** You cannot be targeted by direct attacks until you reveal yourself or are discovered.
- **Stunned:** You cannot take actions or reactions until the end of your next turn.
- **Slowed:** Your movement is halved and you cannot Dash.

Conditions are typically cleared by taking a specific action to resolve them, receiving aid from an ally, or at the end of an encounter.

### Breaking
When you mark your last Stress box, you are **Broken**. When you are Broken:
1. You immediately drop to 0 HP, regardless of your current HP.
2. You must make a Death Move (see the Core Rules).
3. You cannot mark any more Stress. If an effect would cause you to mark Stress while Broken, you take 1d6 direct damage instead (ignoring Armor).

You remain Broken until you complete a Long Rest or receive specialized medical/psychiatric care.

chapters.title: Chapter 13: Rest and Recovery
chapters.content:
Surviving the frontier requires knowing when to push forward and when to fall back and patch your wounds.

### Short Rests
A Short Rest represents catching your breath, reloading weapons, and applying field dressings. It takes about 15 to 30 minutes of uninterrupted downtime. During a Short Rest, you may:
- Spend Hope to heal HP (1 Hope = 1d4 HP).
- Spend Hope to clear Stress (1 Hope = 1 Stress).
- Attempt to repair one Armor slot (requires a Difficulty 14 Knowledge or Agility roll and appropriate tools).

### Long Rests
A Long Rest represents a full sleep cycle (6 to 8 hours) in a safe, secure location — such as your ship's quarters or a rented hab-unit on Nexus 9. During a Long Rest, you:
- Heal all lost HP.
- Clear all marked Stress.
- Repair all broken Armor slots.
- Clear all negative Conditions.
- Reset any abilities that recharge on a Long Rest.

**The Cost of Resting:** In the volatile political climate of Nexus 9, time is a resource. Taking a Long Rest often means advancing the GM's Threat Clocks. While you sleep, the Syndicate moves contraband, the Aurelians fortify their positions, and the Eclipse corruption spreads further into new sectors.

chapters.title: 1. The Session Zero Framework

sections.title: 1.1 Safety Tools
sections.content:
The universe of *Nexus 9* is dangerous, but the gaming table must be safe. The GM should implement the following tools:

*   **Lines and Veils:** Before character creation, every player (including the GM) lists their Lines (topics that will absolutely not appear in the game) and Veils (topics that may exist in the world but will only be described in a "fade to black" manner). Common Veils in *Nexus 9* might include torture, explicit gore, or harm to children.
*   **The X-Card:** A physical card (or digital equivalent) placed in the center of the table. If any player touches or invokes the X-Card, the current scene immediately stops and the narrative is edited to remove the offending content, no questions asked.
*   **Open Door Policy:** Any player can leave the table at any time for any reason without judgment.

sections.title: 1.2 Tone Calibration
sections.content:
*Nexus 9* can be played in several different ways. The group should discuss and agree on the campaign's primary tone:

| Tone | Description | Primary Focus |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **The Diplomatic Corps** | High-society intrigue, embassy parties, and preventing war through words. | Social conflict, faction reputation, espionage. |
| **The Underbelly** | Smuggling, black markets, gang wars, and surviving the Grey Ring. | Heists, street-level combat, debt management. |
| **The Frontier Patrol** | Exploring derelicts, fighting pirates, and investigating ancient ruins. | Ship combat, exploration, cosmic horror. |
| **The Vanguard** | Hunting Eclipse-Corrupted agents and preparing for the coming war. | Investigation, tactical combat, uncovering secrets. |

sections.title: 1.3 The Campaign Premise
sections.content:
Once the tone is set, the GM should present the core premise of the campaign. If using the official *Gathering Storm* campaign book, the premise is:

> *"You are an independent crew operating out of Nexus 9. You are deeply in debt, politically unaffiliated, and desperate for work. The fragile peace between the four major powers is cracking, and you are about to be caught in the middle."*

---

chapters.content:
*Nexus 9* is a game of political intrigue, moral ambiguity, and cosmic horror. Because the setting deals with themes of war, corruption, prejudice, and sacrifice, establishing a strong foundation of trust and shared expectations before the dice hit the table is critical.

Session Zero is a dedicated meeting where the GM and players collaboratively build the campaign's tone, establish safety tools, and create the crew as a cohesive unit.

chapters.title: 2. Crew Creation

sections.title: Step 1: The Ship
sections.content:
Unless the campaign specifically dictates otherwise, the crew begins with a starship. This ship is their home, their transport, and their largest financial liability.

The group must collectively choose one of the following starting frames:

| Frame | Best For | Starting HP | Starting Evasion |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Hermes-Class Courier** | Diplomacy, speed, running blockades. | 20 | 12 |
| **Orion-Class Freighter** | Smuggling, salvage, heavy cargo. | 30 | 10 |
| **Scarab-Class Gunship** | Bounty hunting, mercenary work, combat. | 40 | 9 |

**Name the Ship:** The crew must agree on a name for their vessel.
**The Quirk:** Every used ship has a quirk. Roll 1d6:
1. The life support smells faintly of ozone and old copper.
2. The comms array occasionally picks up encrypted Mindclave static.
3. The artificial gravity in the cargo bay stutters during hyperspace jumps.
4. The ship's registry is flagged in the Aurelian Empire (expect frequent stops).
5. There is a sealed, welded-shut compartment near the engine core.
6. The ship's AI has a sarcastic, slightly morbid personality.

sections.title: Step 2: The Debt
sections.content:
No one gets a ship for free. The crew begins the game owing a massive debt (typically 500,000 Credits) to a powerful entity. This debt is the primary engine that drives the crew to take dangerous jobs.

Roll 1d6 or choose the Crew's Creditor:

| d6 | The Creditor | The Catch |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1** | **The Kaelen Syndicate** | They expect you to move illegal cargo no questions asked. |
| **2** | **A Terran Megacorporation** | They require you to conduct corporate espionage against rivals. |
| **3** | **An Aurelian Noble** | They demand you transport them (or their illicit goods) discreetly. |
| **4** | **Jax (Underbelly Fixer)** | He takes a 20% cut of every job you do until the debt is paid. |
| **5** | **The Vanguard** | The ship is technically theirs; you are expected to drop everything when they call. |
| **6** | **A Technoseer Magus** | They don't want Credits; they want Progenitor artifacts. |

sections.title: Step 3: Crew Roles
sections.content:
Every ship needs a crew. The players must decide who fills which primary role during starship combat and exploration. A character's Class often dictates their best role, but anyone can attempt any job.

*   **The Helm (Pilot/Operative):** Controls movement, evasion, and pursuit.
*   **Tactical (Marine/Engineer):** Controls weapons, targeting, and suppressive fire.
*   **Engineering (Engineer/Medic):** Manages power routing, emergency repairs, and shields.
*   **Sensors/Comms (Mystic/Envoy):** Manages scanning, electronic warfare, and communications.
*   **Quartermaster (Broker):** Manages cargo, fuel, and bribes (used primarily out of combat).

---

chapters.content: In *Nexus 9*, you do not create individual characters in a vacuum. You create a **Crew**. The Crew has its own resources, its own history, and its own problems.

chapters.title: 3. Character Creation & Relationship Mapping

sections.title: 3.1 The Bonds of the Crew
sections.content:
Every character must establish a **Bond** with at least two other characters in the crew. These bonds represent past experiences, shared trauma, or mutual reliance.

Players take turns choosing a prompt from the table below and assigning it to another player's character. The two players then briefly discuss the details of that shared history.

| d12 | Relationship Prompt |
|:---:|:---|
| **1** | You saved my life during a firefight in the Grey Ring. I owe you, and I hate owing people. |
| **2** | We both know a dangerous secret about the Aurelian Empire. If they find out we know, we're dead. |
| **3** | You caught me stealing from the ship's stores, but you didn't tell the others. Why? |
| **4** | We served on opposite sides of a border skirmish before joining this crew. The tension is still there. |
| **5** | You are the only one who knows about my telepathic sensitivity (or my lack thereof). |
| **6** | We spent a week stranded in an escape pod together. We know each other's deepest fears. |
| **7** | I blame you for the job that went wrong and put us in this massive debt. |
| **8** | You remind me of someone I lost during the Eclipse Awakening. I am overly protective of you. |
| **9** | We share a contact in the Underbelly who only trusts the two of us. |
| **10** | I suspect you are secretly working for the Vanguard, but I haven't confronted you yet. |
| **11** | We both lost our homes to the Kaelen Syndicate. We share a burning desire for revenge. |
| **12** | You are the only reason I haven't abandoned this crew and this station. |

sections.title: 3.2 The Secret
sections.content:
In addition to Bonds, every character must have one **Secret**. This is something they are actively hiding from the rest of the crew. The GM should know this secret and use it to complicate the narrative later in the campaign.

Players can invent their own secret or roll on the table below:

| d8 | The Secret |
|:---:|:---|
| **1** | I am a sleeper agent for the Mindclave, reporting on the crew's activities. |
| **2** | I am not who I say I am; I stole this identity to escape a massive bounty. |
| **3** | I am infected with a dormant strain of Eclipse corruption. I can hear them whispering. |
| **4** | I know the location of a pristine Progenitor artifact, but I need the crew's help to get it. |
| **5** | I am secretly a noble of the Aurelian Empire, hiding from assassins. |
| **6** | I murdered the previous owner of our ship. |
| **7** | I am addicted to a rare, highly illegal combat stim, and my supply is running low. |
| **8** | I have a family hidden in the Grey Ring, and all my share of the Credits goes to keeping them safe. |

chapters.content:
Once the Crew's foundation (the ship, the debt, and the roles) is established, players create their individual characters using the standard *Nexus 9* rules (Ancestry, Community, Class, and Subclass).

However, a crew that has been flying together already has history. To simulate this, the group engages in **Relationship Mapping**.

chapters.title: 4. Campaign Integration
chapters.content:
Once the Crew is built, the GM should take a few minutes to integrate their choices into the first session of the campaign.

*   **The Debt Collector:** The first NPC the crew interacts with should be a representative of their Creditor, demanding a payment or offering a dangerous job to clear a portion of the debt.
*   **The Ship's Quirk:** The GM should trigger the ship's quirk during the first high-stress situation (e.g., the gravity stutters just as a firefight breaks out in the cargo bay).
*   **The Bonds:** The GM should present situations that test the established bonds. If two characters share a secret about the Aurelian Empire, the first job should involve an Aurelian noble.

By the end of Session Zero, the players should feel like a cohesive unit with a shared history, a clear motivation (the debt), and a tangible connection to the dangerous universe of *Nexus 9*.

---

*End of Session Zero & Crew Creation Toolkit*

chapters.title: Character Creation
chapters.content:
## Building Your Agent

Creating a character in *Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* follows a structured, step-by-step process. Each step builds on the last, producing a fully realized operative ready to face the dangers of the Known Galaxy. This chapter walks you through every decision, from choosing your ancestry to recording your starting gear.

Before you begin, you will need a character sheet (see Appendix), two twelve-sided dice (2d12), and a set of standard polyhedral dice (d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20).

---

## Step 1: Choose Your Ancestry

Your Ancestry represents the species or cultural lineage your character was born into. It determines your physical traits, your starting language, and one innate ability that reflects your heritage.

Nexus 9 features the following Ancestries:

| Ancestry | Description | Ancestry Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Terran** | Humans from the Coalition core worlds. Adaptable, ambitious, and widespread. | *Tenacity:* Once per Long Rest, when you fail an action roll, you may immediately reroll it and take the new result. |
| **Aurelian** | Tall, long-lived humanoids with gilded skin and a culture of imperial grandeur. | *Imperial Bearing:* You gain Advantage on Presence rolls when interacting with anyone who recognizes your noble heritage. |
| **Kaelen** | Stocky, resilient humanoids with mottled grey-green skin, adapted to harsh frontier worlds. | *Born Survivor:* You gain Advantage on Instinct rolls to resist environmental hazards (extreme cold, radiation, low oxygen). |
| **Valari** | Slender, crystalline-boned humanoids with luminous eyes and a deep spiritual tradition. | *Crystal Resonance:* You can sense the presence of Progenitor technology or Eclipse corruption within Close range without a roll. |
| **Mindclave-Born** | Terrans or Aurelians born with latent telepathic potential, identified and raised within the Mindclave. | *Psychic Sensitivity:* You can read the surface emotions (not thoughts) of any creature within Very Close range as a Free Action. |
| **Frontier-Born** | Mixed-heritage individuals from the unaligned border worlds — scrappy, resourceful, and self-reliant. | *Resourceful:* Once per Short Rest, you may produce a common, non-weapon item worth 50 Credits or less that you "happened to have." |
| **Synthetic** | Artificial beings — androids, uploaded consciousnesses, or bio-mechanical constructs. | *Machine Logic:* You are immune to telepathic intrusion and the Disoriented condition. You do not need to eat, drink, or breathe, but you require maintenance during Long Rests. |

---

## Step 2: Choose Your Community

Your Community represents where you grew up and the values that shaped you. It provides one Experience (a narrative skill bonus) and one Community Feature.

| Community | Description | Starting Experience | Community Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Core Worlder** | Raised on a wealthy, well-connected inner world. | Bureaucracy (+2) | *Connections:* You know the name and location of one mid-ranking official in any major faction. |
| **Station Rat** | Grew up on Nexus 9 or a similar orbital habitat. | Street Smarts (+2) | *Station Knowledge:* You always know the fastest route between any two points on a station, and you can identify safe houses and bolt-holes without a roll. |
| **Frontier Settler** | Raised on a remote colony world with little infrastructure. | Survival (+2) | *Self-Reliant:* During a Short Rest, you can repair one piece of damaged equipment without tools or an Engineer. |
| **Military Brat** | Grew up on Coalition military bases and warships. | Tactics (+2) | *Drilled:* In the first round of any combat, you act before any adversary, regardless of the spotlight order. |
| **Underbelly Dweller** | Raised in the criminal underworld of a major station. | Deception (+2) | *Underworld Contacts:* Once per session, you can locate a black-market dealer, fence, or informant in your current location without a roll. |
| **Temple Raised** | Raised in a Valari monastery or Aurelian religious order. | Theology (+2) | *Inner Peace:* Once per Long Rest, you may clear 2 Stress without spending Hope or taking a rest action. |
| **Exile** | You were cast out from your original community — a political dissident, a criminal, or a refugee. | Persuasion (+2) | *Nothing Left to Lose:* When you are at 3 or fewer HP, you gain +1 to all action rolls. |
| **Technoseer Apprentice** | Trained (briefly) by the Technoseers before leaving or being expelled. | Engineering (+2) | *Progenitor Intuition:* You gain Advantage on Knowledge rolls to identify, activate, or repair Progenitor technology. |

---

## Step 3: Choose Your Class

Your Class defines your core combat role, your primary abilities, and the Domain cards available to you. Nexus 9 features eight classes, each adapted from the Daggerheart core framework.

Choose one of the following classes. Full class descriptions, subclass features, and Domain card lists are found in Book Three.

| Class | Role | Domains | Starting HP | Starting Evasion |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Envoy** | Face / Social Specialist | Grace & Codex | 5 | 10 |
| **Operative** | Striker / Infiltrator | Midnight & Grace | 6 | 12 |
| **Pilot** | Skirmisher / Vehicle Specialist | Bone & Sage | 6 | 12 |
| **Marine** | Frontline Combatant | Blade & Bone | 6 | 11 |
| **Engineer** | Protector / Support Tank | Valor & Blade | 7 | 9 |
| **Mystic** | Ranged Damage / Telepathy | Arcana & Midnight | 6 | 10 |
| **Broker** | Utility / Controller | Sage & Arcana | 6 | 10 |
| **Medic** | Healer / Support | Splendor & Valor | 7 | 9 |

---

## Step 4: Choose Your Subclass

Each class offers two subclasses. At Level 1, you choose one and gain its Foundation Feature. As you advance, you will unlock its Specialization Feature (mid-levels) and Mastery Feature (high levels). See Book Three for the full subclass feature descriptions.

---

## Step 5: Assign Your Traits

Nexus 9 uses six Traits that define your character's physical and mental capabilities:

| Trait | Governs |
|:---|:---|
| **Agility** | Speed, reflexes, ranged accuracy, dodging |
| **Strength** | Raw physical power, melee force, endurance |
| **Finesse** | Precision, sleight of hand, stealth, lockpicking |
| **Instinct** | Awareness, gut feelings, survival, tracking |
| **Presence** | Charisma, intimidation, leadership, deception |
| **Knowledge** | Education, technology, medicine, history |

### Trait Generation: Standard Array

Assign the following values to your six Traits in any order:

**+2, +1, +1, 0, 0, -1**

This array ensures every character has clear strengths and a meaningful weakness. Your class description in Book Three recommends which Traits to prioritize, but you are free to assign them however you wish.

### Trait Generation: Point-Buy (Optional)

If your group prefers more customization, use the Point-Buy method:

1. All Traits start at **-1**.
